cases.title: A fact changed between sessions — return the current value, never the stale one

seed.statement: Later update: the user now works at Google.

turns.prompt: just settling in at the new place

cases.note: The employer changed between sessions. Temporal actuality: the latest observation is the answer, and the superseded value must be silently retired — not surfaced next to the new one. A store that keeps every version and returns them all by relevance fails, because the agent cannot tell which employer is current.

cases.title: A newer observation retires an older one by time, without any stale flag

seed.statement: The release is planned for June.
The release date was moved to August.

cases.note: Neither fact carries a status:stale flag — the June date is outdated purely by time, because a later observation moved it. Freshness must be derived from the temporal relation (newer supersedes older), exactly what a temporal knowledge graph is supposed to do. This is the case that separates temporal-graph memory from a plain vector store, which would return both dates ranked by similarity.

cases.title: A drifting numeric value returns its most recent reading

seed.statement: Most recent: the user weighs 76 kg.

cases.note: A value that changes over time (weight, price, version) must resolve to its latest reading. The old reading is not wrong history — it is just no longer current — so it is retired by supersede rather than deleted. The query returns exactly one number.
